answer:
**The verse you're referring to is the second couplet (sh'er) of the ghazal "Rahiye ab aisi jagah chal kar jahan koi na ho" from Mirza Ghalib's *Diwan-E-Ghalib* (chapter 168).**[^1]

In the original Urdu (right-to-left):  
بے_در_و_دیوار سا اک گھر بنایا چاہیے  
کوئی ہمسایہ نہ ہو اور پاسباں کوئی نہ ہو[^2]

Transliteration:  
Be-dar-o-deewar sa ek ghar banaya chahiye  
Koi humsaya na ho aur pasban koi na ho[^3]

English translation:  
A house I'll build of neither wall nor stone,  
No neighbor there, no guard, and be alone.[^1]

Here, *humsaya* (ہمسایہ) conveys "neighbor" or "companion/co-resident," while *pasban* (پاسباں) means "door-keeper," "sentry," or "guard." Ghalib expresses a desire for utter isolation in this wall-less house, free from neighbors, companions, and gatekeepers. The first couplet similarly evokes solitude ("no kindred voice, no soul to call my own"), but the specific phrasing matches the second.[^4]

[^1]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 168 "Rahiye ab aisi jagah chal kar jahan koi na ho" [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/168-rahiye-ab-aisi-jagah-chal-kar-jahan-koi-na-ho/translation/#p-09bdfe).
[^2]: Ibid., [original](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/168-rahiye-ab-aisi-jagah-chal-kar-jahan-koi-na-ho/original/#p-a28967).
[^3]: Ibid., [transliteration](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/168-rahiye-ab-aisi-jagah-chal-kar-jahan-koi-na-ho/transliteration/#p-198d6d).
[^4]: Ibid., full chapter [translation](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/168-rahiye-ab-aisi-jagah-chal-kar-jahan-koi-na-ho/translation/).

args.query: Rahiye ab aisi jagah chal kar jahan koi na ho
